risks.title: Engineering layer lineweights truncated to 0 by int() in layer_create
risks.detail: apply_layer_set passes float mm lineweights from ENGINEERING_LAYERS (0.50, 0.25, 0.18, 0.13, 0.05) into backend.layer_create(lineweight=...), which does int(lineweight) (ezdxf_backend.py:1034). All sub-1.0 values floor to 0, so EVERY engineering layer is written with lineweight 0 in the DXF instead of its ISO value. ezdxf expects integer hundredths-of-mm (50=0.50mm) or AutoCAD enum values, not raw mm. Net effect: the entire ISO-128 lineweight discipline that CLAUDE.md advertises is silently lost in output, and drawing_apply_iso_layers/drawing_new bootstrap produce flat 0-weight layers.

risks.title: drawing_critique iso128 check is effectively dead because lineweights are 0 and units are mis-asserted
risks.detail: _check_iso128 reads LayerInfo.lineweight (set to float(raw_dxf_lineweight) at ezdxf_backend.py:189 — NOT normalised to mm, contradicting the comment at critique.py:53 'the layer dataclass already normalises to mm'). Combined with the int()-truncation bug above, every layer reports lw<=0 and is skipped at critique.py:50, so the ISO-128 lineweight gate can never fire. If lineweights were ever stored correctly as hundredths (e.g. 25), the check compares 25 against the mm set {0.13..2.0} and only the lw/100.0 fallback saves it — the unit handling is unverified/fragile.

risks.title: dim_overlap critique never finds dimensions on the ezdxf backend (property names mismatch)
risks.detail: _check_dim_overlap reads e.properties['text_position'] / ['defpoint'] / ['insertion_point']. The ezdxf backend's property extractor for DIM* types (ezdxf_backend.py:166-168) only sets props['dim_type'] and none of those three keys, so dim_points stays empty and the check is a silent no-op. Additionally ezdxf's add_linear_dim produces a DIMENSION entity (group/anonymous block); the extractor branch keys on DIMLINEAR/DIMALIGNED/etc., which may not match the reported dxftype, so even dim_type may be absent. The overlap check cannot fire as wired.

risks.title: Involute flank prepends a radial jump from root to base circle (no trochoid/fillet)
risks.detail: generate_involute_flank appends (root_r, 0.0) then jumps to the involute starting at (base_r, 0.0). Between root_r and base_r the true tooth flank is a trochoidal fillet, not a straight radial segment at angle 0. The produced polyline draws a straight spoke from root to base then the involute — geometrically the dedendum region is wrong and the corner at base radius is a kink. For root_r<base_r (common with x=0 profile shift, 20deg PA, low tooth counts) this misrepresents the tooth root. Acceptable as a schematic but not 'production-quality' as CLAUDE.md claims.

risks.title: Tooth flank rotation uses involute-at-pressure-angle as a proxy for involute-at-pitch-radius
risks.detail: rot = -inv_pitch + theta_pitch with inv_pitch = tan(pa) - pa. The involute function evaluated at the pitch circle for a standard gear is indeed inv(pa)=tan(pa)-pa, so this is correct ONLY for the standard (unshifted) case where the base-circle involute reaches the pitch radius exactly at the pressure angle. t_pitch is computed (gear.py:94) but then unused — the code relies on the analytic inv(pa) instead. The dangling t_pitch computation signals intent to do something more general that was never finished; tooth symmetry/thickness will be slightly off if assumptions (full-depth, no profile shift) are violated, and there is no assertion that the involute sample actually crosses pitch_r.

risks.title: Root arc between teeth assumes root_r >= 0 and base-flank root point lies on root circle
risks.detail: generate_full_gear_outline builds the inter-tooth root arc using a_root_left/a_root_right taken from base_right[0]. When root_r < base_r, base_right[0] is (root_r,0) rotated by rot, so it lies on root_r — OK. But when root_r >= base_r (high tooth counts / large PA), generate_involute_flank returns points starting at (base_r,0) (root point NOT prepended, gear.py:40 guard), so base_right[0] is on base_r not root_r, yet the root arc is drawn at radius root_r (gear.py:189) — the arc endpoints will not coincide with the flank start, leaving a gap/overlap in the closed outline. Also root_r = pitch_r - 1.25*module can be <=0 for teeth<=2, producing a degenerate/negative-radius arc with no guard.

risks.title: Tooth tip arc sampled with only 5 points and endpoint-stripping can drop the tip on coarse gears
risks.detail: tip_arc uses n=5 then [1:-1] is taken (3 interior points). For a thin tooth the tip arc spans a tiny angle and 3 samples is coarse; more importantly generate_tooth_profile (gear.py:117-121) uses tip_arc[1:-1] but includes the full right_flank and reversed left_flank whose last points ARE the tip endpoints, so the tip is represented — but generate_tooth_profile is itself unwired (see smells). In generate_full_gear_outline the same [1:-1] slice is fine. Low risk, but tip fidelity is fixed at 3 interior points regardless of module.

risks.title: untrimmed_corner critique is O(n^2) over all line endpoints up to 5000 lines
risks.detail: Builds up to 2*5000=10000 endpoints and does a nested loop (~5e7 distance comparisons worst case) every critique run. A full gear outline is a single polyline so it is exempt, but title blocks + sections generate many LINE entities; on a busy drawing this check is slow and runs on the single COM STA thread or via to_thread. Functional but a latency risk at the 5000-line limit.

risks.title: Validator title check substring 'HELICAL SPUR' / 'SPUR' is brittle and false-positive prone
risks.detail: Joins all TEXT/MTEXT on the TEXT layer and substring-matches 'HELICAL SPUR' and 'SPUR'. Any annotation legitimately containing 'SPUR' (e.g. a note) on a helical part triggers an error finding that blocks drawing_finalize. Also only fires when expected['helix_angle'] is truthy, so a helical gear finalized without expected metadata is never checked. Heuristic, not load-bearing, but can wrongly fail finalize.

risks.title: DIN 6885 lookup boundary condition has redundant/confusing guard
risks.detail: Loop condition `b_min < bore <= b_max or (bore == b_min and b_min == TABLE[0][0])`. The second clause only special-cases bore==6 (the very first b_min) because the half-open intervals use b_min < bore. Any other exact b_min boundary (e.g. bore==8, ==10, ==12...) falls into the PREVIOUS row's b_max (8 matched by row (6,8,...) since 6<8<=8), which is the intended DIN behaviour, but the special clause for only the first row is asymmetric and easy to misread; a bore exactly equal to a non-first b_min silently maps to the lower row. Worth a comment/test.

smells.title: section.py (cutting_plane_line, apply_section_hatch) is entirely unwired/dead
smells.detail: Both functions are exported via __init__.py:35,65-66 but grep of server.py and backends finds zero references outside the engineering package and no tests. No MCP tool exposes cutting_plane_line or apply_section_hatch. draw_gear_section_aa builds its own hatch inline (gear.py:385) rather than calling apply_section_hatch, and section A-A draws no cutting-plane line at all. The whole module is reachable only via direct import that nothing performs — dead code.

smells.title: generate_tooth_profile is unwired and untested
smells.detail: Exported (__init__.py:9,59) but no caller: drawing uses generate_full_gear_outline instead, and test_engineering_gear.py imports involute_xy/generate_involute_flank/generate_full_gear_outline but NOT generate_tooth_profile. It duplicates ~80% of generate_full_gear_outline's single-tooth math (gear.py:81-117 vs 137-161). Dead/duplicated single-tooth path.

smells.title: Duplicated involute/rot/radii math between generate_tooth_profile and generate_full_gear_outline
smells.detail: pitch_r/base_r/outer_r/root_r, theta_pitch, inv_pitch, rot, base_right, base_left, tip-arc-angle blocks are copy-pasted between the two functions. Any fix to the flank math (e.g. the root-trochoid bug) must be applied twice; they have already diverged (generate_tooth_profile computes unused t_pitch at gear.py:94, the outline does not).

smells.title: COM backend stubs out drawing_plan/critique/apply_iso_layers/construction_* — premium workflow is ezdxf-only
smells.detail: drawing_plan (Task #8), drawing_critique (Task #8), construction_xline/clear (Task #7), drawing_apply_iso_layers (Task #9) all raise NotImplementedError on the COM backend. CLAUDE.md presents the premium plan->apply_iso_layers->critique->finalize workflow as the production path, but on live AutoCAD (the COM backend) half of it is unimplemented. drawing_finalize/DrawingValidator and the gear/keyway/titleblock draw tools DO work on COM (they only use generic entity_create_* / entity_list), but the planning+critique gate does not. Doc-vs-code drift / parity gap.

smells.title: iso128 critique comment claims layer lineweight is normalised to mm; it is not
smells.detail: Comment: 'the layer dataclass already normalises to mm'. _layer_info_dxf stores float(lw) of the raw DXF lineweight with no /100 normalisation (ezdxf_backend.py:184-189). The comment is false and masks the lineweight-unit confusion driving the iso128 dead-check risk above.

smells.title: draw_keyed_bore intentionally creates an untrimmed-looking notch that drawing_critique would not flag but isn't trimmed into the bore
smells.detail: The keyway is an open 4-point polyline laid over the full bore circle; the bore circle is NOT trimmed at the keyway mouth, so the circle arc still crosses the keyway opening. This is a common schematic convention but contradicts CLAUDE.md rule 5 ('corners must be explicit', use entity_trim). The untrimmed_corner critique only inspects LINE-LINE endpoints (critique.py:116) so it cannot see the circle/polyline overlap — the inconsistency goes unflagged.

smells.title: TitleBlockMetadata.company defaults to a hardcoded vendor name 'Anka-Makine'
smells.detail: company: str = 'Anka-Makine' bakes a specific company into the generic title-block default. Any drawing finalized without overriding company silently stamps this name. Should default to empty string or be configuration-driven.

smells.title: Title text horizontal centering is a crude char-count heuristic
smells.detail: title_x = center - len(title)*TITLE_HEIGHT*0.3 estimates string width from character count, which is wrong for proportional fonts and will mis-center long/short titles. Cosmetic but undermines the 'production-quality deterministic' claim.

smells.title: __init__.py guards titleblock/validator imports behind try/except ImportError 'while Agent B is in flight'
smells.detail: TitleBlockMetadata/apply_iso_a3_titleblock/DrawingValidator/etc. are set to None on ImportError, but both modules now exist and are imported unconditionally by server.py (server.py:2266,2294). If those modules raise a real ImportError (e.g. a typo), the package silently degrades the symbols to None and server.py's 'from engineering import ... TitleBlockMetadata' would import None instead of failing loudly. Stale scaffolding comment + error-swallowing.

smells.title: PID_LAYERS / ISO13567_LAYERS reference linetypes DASHED/DASHDOT loaded by ensure_standard_linetypes, but mech path also loads them unnecessarily
smells.detail: STANDARD_LINETYPES includes DASHED and DASHDOT which only the pid/iso13567 sets use, yet apply_layer_set always calls ensure_standard_linetypes (layers.py:110) for every set including 'mech'. Minor: loads 2 unused linetypes for mechanical drawings. Harmless but indicates the linetype set isn't keyed to the chosen layer set.

moduleMaps.notes: Wiring verdict (grep of server.py + backends): WIRED to MCP tools = draw_helical_gear_front_view, draw_spur_gear_front_view, draw_gear_section_aa, draw_keyed_bore, draw_keyway_section, apply_iso_a3_titleblock+TitleBlockMetadata, DrawingValidator, PlanSpec (via ezdxf drawing_plan), run_critique (via ezdxf drawing_critique), apply_layer_set/ensure_engineering_layers (via drawing_apply_iso_layers + drawing_new bootstrap). Pure-math involute_xy/generate_involute_flank/generate_full_gear_outline are used transitively by the gear draw tools and covered by tests. UNWIRED/DEAD = section.py cutting_plane_line & apply_section_hatch (no references outside engineering/, no tool, no test) and gear.py generate_tooth_profile (exported but no caller, no test, duplicates outline math). PARITY GAP = drawing_plan/drawing_critique/drawing_apply_iso_layers/construction_* raise NotImplementedError on the COM backend (com_backend.py:1637-1654), so the entire 'premium workflow' planning+critique gate only functions on ezdxf, despite CLAUDE.md presenting it as the production path. Highest-impact bug to verify first: int()-truncation of mm lineweights in layer_create (ezdxf_backend.py:1034) which nullifies the ISO-128 lineweight system AND makes the iso128 critique a permanent no-op. Did not run the test suite; findings are from static reading of the cited files.

moduleMaps.module: tests/ (test suite) — coverage map for AutoCAD MCP Pro
moduleMaps.purpose: Audit of the pytest suite: a single shared ezdxf-backed `backend` fixture (tests/conftest.py) drives 14 test files. The suite exercises the ezdxf backend, the security sanitizers (in isolation), the engineering package (gear/keyway/titleblock/validator), corner ops (trim/extend/fillet/chamfer), and the premium meta-tools (plan/critique/snap/construction/iso-layers/dimension_auto/select_smart). The COM backend, the server-layer MCP tool wiring, and several backend methods are entirely untested.
public_surface: conftest.py::backend fixture — EzdxfBackend + connect + drawing_new (the ONLY fixture; no COM fixture exists)
test_ezdxf_backend.py (16): CRUD, type-filter exact match, rotate/scale/mirror/array transforms, transaction commit+rollback, block_create_from_entities

test_meta_tools.py (32): drawing_plan, drawing_critique (3 of 6 focuses), point_from_snap (all snaps), construction_*, drawing_apply_iso_layers (mech/pid/iso13567), dimension_auto (chain/baseline only), entity_select_smart, plan->draw->critique pipeline
test_security.py (29): validate_path, sanitize_command, sanitize_lisp — all in isolation, never via server tools
test_entity_corner_ops.py (18): trim/extend/fillet/chamfer ezdxf-only (header explicitly skips COM)
key_flows: TEST COUNT: 184 test functions actually collected (pytest --collect-only reports '184 tests collected'), across 14 test files — NOT 13. README.md:83 claims '104 tests'; the real number is 184 (README understates by 80).
All async tests share ONE function-scoped ezdxf fixture (tests/conftest.py:8-15); every test re-creates a fresh in-memory DXF doc. No COM fixture exists anywhere.

Security is tested only at the pure-function level (security.py). The server.py MCP tool wiring (server.py:2035 sanitize_command, :2052 sanitize_lisp, :276/:306/:324/:340/:354/:368/:2298/:2302 validate_path) has NO integration test proving the tools actually invoke the sanitizers.
COM backend (backends/com_backend.py, ~80 async methods) has ZERO coverage — no test imports ComBackend/win32com; test_entity_corner_ops.py header states COM tests are skipped.
Engineering section module (engineering/section.py: cutting_plane_line, apply_section_hatch) has ZERO coverage.
dependencies: pytest-cov>=5.0 (declared but NO coverage gate / addopts configured)
ezdxf (only backend the suite can exercise)

risks.title: COM backend has zero automated coverage
risks.location: backends/com_backend.py:374-1659 (entire file); tests/ has no ComBackend import
risks.detail: ~80 async COM methods including transaction_begin/commit/rollback (com_backend.py:1396-1422 use StartUndoMark/EndUndoMark/SendCommand '_UNDO B'), entity_trim/extend/fillet/chamfer via SendCommand string-building (1549+), and the CMDACTIVE deadlock-guard logic (_safe_send_command, 1497-1547) are never tested. This is the production path on Windows. The undo-mark transaction model is semantically different from the ezdxf snapshot model, so the passing ezdxf transaction tests (test_ezdxf_backend.py:149-168) give false confidence that transactions work on COM.

risks.title: Server-layer security wiring is unverified (sanitizer-bypass blind spot)
risks.detail: test_security.py tests sanitize_command/sanitize_lisp/validate_path as standalone functions but never asserts that the MCP tools system_run_command/system_run_lisp/drawing_* actually call them. If a future refactor drops the sanitize_command(command) call at server.py:2035, every security test still passes while raw commands reach AutoCAD's SendCommand (com_backend.py:1483). The highest-value security guarantee (tool -> sanitizer -> backend) is the one thing not covered.

risks.title: LISP/command sanitizer bypass edge-cases untested
risks.detail: The code comment at security.py:138-140 explicitly claims to defend against aliasing tricks like '(setq f command)(f "ERASE")', but NO test exercises that bypass. Also untested: command sanitizer word-boundary evasion (e.g. dot-prefixed '.ERASE', leading-underscore variants beyond the few listed, concatenated/whitespace-padded payloads), UNC paths (\\server\share) and '~' home expansion in validate_path, and symlink escape from ALLOWED_PATHS. The regex-based denylist is exactly the kind of thing that needs adversarial test cases and has none.

risks.title: Critique focuses iso128 / layer_color / dim_overlap failure paths untested
risks.detail: drawing_critique exposes 6 focuses (critique.py:220-225). Tests only assert the positive-failure path for construction_left, untrimmed_corner, duplicate_entities (test_meta_tools.py). iso128, layer_color, and dim_overlap are only exercised via the clean-drawing case (test_critique_clean_drawing, :40-44) which asserts they DON'T fire — their actual detection logic is never proven to catch a violation. The unknown-focus error branch (critique.py:241-242) is also untested.

risks.title: dimension_auto 'ordinate' style untested
risks.detail: dimension_auto supports chain/baseline/ordinate (ezdxf_backend.py:1929). Tests cover chain (test_chain_creates_one_dim_per_segment) and baseline, plus the unknown-style raise and empty-handles cases, but the ordinate branch (1988+, the most complex — marks X then Y of each endpoint) has no test.

risks.title: Aligned/angular dimensions permanently xfail — feature effectively unverified
risks.detail: dimension_aligned and dimension_angular are marked xfail(strict=False) with 'ezdxf API parameter mismatch'. These are advertised MCP tools (server section 3, 5 dimension_* tools). They are shipped broken in the ezdxf backend with the test acknowledging the breakage rather than fixing it; strict=False means even an accidental fix would not flip to a failure to alert anyone.

risks.title: Multiple backend methods have no test at any level
risks.location: backends/ezdxf_backend.py / com_backend.py: system_get_variable(1451), system_set_variable(1457), system_status(1426), drawing_close(552), drawing_export_pdf(528), entity_create_block_ref(722), block_set_attributes(1239), linetype_list(1112), linetype_load(1118), view_zoom_extents/window(1371/1379), view_screenshot(1386)
risks.detail: Grep of tests/ for these method names returns zero hits. linetype_load is load-bearing for the engineering linetype bootstrap; view_screenshot is the matplotlib render path for ezdxf; drawing_export_pdf is an advertised tool. All are uncovered on both backends.

risks.title: Transaction nesting / error semantics untested
risks.detail: Only single begin->create->rollback and begin->create->commit are tested. Nested transactions, rollback-without-begin, commit-without-begin, and rollback restoring layers/blocks (not just entities) are untested. The ezdxf snapshot stack (_undo_stack per CLAUDE.md) could mishandle nesting and the suite would not detect it.

smells.title: README test count is stale/wrong (104 vs actual 184)
smells.detail: README claims '104 tests across drawing, entity, dimension, layer, block, analysis, batch/template, security suites'. pytest --collect-only collects 184. The README list also omits the engineering, corner-ops, and meta-tools suites entirely (which together add ~80 tests). Doc-vs-code drift.

smells.title: Security tests mutate global config.settings with fragile manual restore
smells.detail: test_allowed_paths_enforcement and the *_bypass tests reassign module-global config.settings (config.settings = Settings()) and rely on manual teardown lines to restore it. test_allowed_paths_enforcement (84-88) even does config.settings = Settings.__new__(Settings) then sets attributes by hand. If any assertion mid-test raises, the global is left polluted and can cross-contaminate other tests depending on collection order. Should use a fixture with guaranteed teardown / monkeypatch.setattr on config.settings.

smells.title: Defensive 'if start:' / 'if start and end:' guards hide assertion skips
smells.detail: test_batch_modify_move asserts coordinates only 'if start:' — if properties.get('start') is empty the test passes without checking anything (vacuous pass). Same in test_validation_zero_length_detection ('if start and end:'). test_analysis_entity_stats has a deeply nested .get() fallback chain that makes the assertion 'total >= 3' nearly unfalsifiable. These tests can stay green while the underlying property serialization breaks.

smells.title: Many 'template/validation' tests assert nothing about the feature they name
smells.detail: test_template_architectural_layers / _mechanical_ / _electrical_ just call layer_create in a loop and assert the layers exist — there is no 'template' code under test (templates are inlined in the test). test_validation_* reimplement empty-layer/zero-length detection in the test body instead of calling a backend validator. These are testing ezdxf layer_create N times over, not any template or validation feature; low value, misleading names.

smells.title: Validator clean-drawing test needs a synthetic HIDDEN linetype workaround
smells.detail: _ensure_hidden_linetype reaches into backend._require_doc() and manually adds a HIDDEN linetype pattern because 'ezdxf doesn't ship a HIDDEN pattern'. The test bypasses the public API and patches internal state to make the validator pass, indicating the validator's linetype check and the ezdxf bootstrap (ensure_standard_linetypes) disagree about HIDDEN — the workaround masks that mismatch rather than fixing it.

smells.title: Validator title-check test accepts either of two finding codes
smells.detail: assert 'title_helical_spur' in codes or 'title_says_spur_for_helical' in codes — the test does not pin which code the validator emits, so a refactor could silently change the contract while the test stays green. Indicates code/test ambiguity about the finding's canonical code.

smells.title: No coverage gate despite pytest-cov dependency
smells.location: pyproject.toml:53-54 (only asyncio_mode set); pytest-cov declared at dev deps
smells.detail: pytest-cov>=5.0 is a declared dev dependency but [tool.pytest.ini_options] has no addopts/--cov, no fail-under threshold, and no testpaths. Coverage is never measured in CI (the .github/workflows/ci.yml is also deleted per git status), so the COM/section/critique gaps go unnoticed by tooling.

smells.title: CI workflow deleted — suite not enforced
smells.detail: The only CI workflow is staged for deletion. With no CI, the 184 tests (and the 2 xfail dimension regressions) are not gated on PRs; regressions in untested COM paths or sanitizer wiring would ship undetected.

moduleMaps.notes: Verified counts by hand: pytest --collect-only prints '184 tests collected'; per-file grep of 'def test_' matches (analysis 12, batch_template 9, dimensions 5, drawing 8, gear 9, keyway 5, titleblock 7, validator 9, corner_ops 18, entity_creation 13, ezdxf_backend 16, layer_block 12, meta_tools 32, security 29 = 184). 14 test files (prompt expected ~13). Highest-value untested paths, ranked: (1) COM backend entirely — esp. transactions, run_command/run_lisp SendCommand, corner-ops string-building; (2) server tool -> sanitizer wiring (no integration test); (3) LISP aliasing-bypass + path edge cases the code comments claim to defend; (4) critique iso128/layer_color/dim_overlap detection; (5) dimension_auto ordinate + aligned/angular (xfail). The ezdxf backend itself is reasonably well covered for happy paths.

moduleMaps.module: Documentation-vs-reality drift audit (CLAUDE.md Premium Drawing Rules + README.md claims)
moduleMaps.purpose: Verify that the 12 premium tools referenced in CLAUDE.md's "Premium Drawing Rules" actually exist in server.py + both backends, and that README.md's concrete claims (87 tools, 104 tests, Dockerfile, .env.example, benchmarks/, LICENSE, .github/workflows/ci.yml) match the repo on disk.
public_surface: server.py: 109 @mcp.tool decorators registered (CLAUDE.md/README claim '87 tools' but explicitly defer to system_about for the live count)

backends/base.py: AutoCADBackend ABC declares all 12 premium-rule methods (entity_trim:307, entity_fillet:325, entity_chamfer:335, drawing_plan:513, drawing_critique:527, point_from_snap:535, construction_xline:546, construction_clear:555, drawing_apply_iso_layers:563, dimension_auto:571, entity_select_smart:581)
backends/ezdxf_backend.py: all 12 premium-rule methods implemented with real bodies
backends/com_backend.py: entity_trim/fillet/chamfer implemented via SendCommand; the other 8 premium meta-tools raise NotImplementedError
key_flows: Premium-rule tool resolution: server.py @mcp.tool wrapper -> _backend(ctx).<method>(...) -> ezdxf_backend (full impl) OR com_backend (8 of 12 raise NotImplementedError)

Test coverage path: tests/conftest.py backend fixture instantiates EzdxfBackend ONLY -> tests/test_meta_tools.py exercises all 12 premium tools against ezdxf, never against COM -> COM stubs are untested and silently unverified
README file-existence claims vs repo: LICENSE and benchmarks/ absent; .github/workflows/ci.yml tracked-but-deleted; Dockerfile + .env.example present

risks.title: 8 premium meta-tools raise NotImplementedError on COM backend while docs present them as production-ready
risks.detail: drawing_plan (1637-1639), drawing_critique (1641-1642), point_from_snap (1644-1645), construction_xline (1647-1648), construction_clear (1650-1651), drawing_apply_iso_layers (1653-1654), dimension_auto (1656-1657), entity_select_smart (1659-1660) all `raise NotImplementedError("... pending Task #N")`. CLAUDE.md 'Premium Drawing Rules' calls these 'non-negotiable for production engineering output' and the 'Standard Premium Workflow' template chains drawing_plan->drawing_apply_iso_layers->construction_xline->dimension_auto->drawing_critique->construction_clear. On a live AutoCAD (COM) session — README's primary 'real-time control' use case, listed 'Verified' for Claude Desktop/Cursor — every one of these steps throws. The documented premium workflow is fully functional ONLY on the headless ezdxf backend.

risks.title: COM stubs for premium tools are never tested; test fixture is ezdxf-only
risks.detail: The shared `backend` fixture instantiates `EzdxfBackend()` exclusively (import at line 5, construction at line 11). tests/test_meta_tools.py (32 test functions) and all other suites run only against ezdxf, so the COM NotImplementedError stubs at com_backend.py:1637-1660 have zero coverage. README line 83 claims '104 tests across ... security suites' and line 102-103 mark COM hosts 'Verified', but no COM code path — including the premium stubs — is exercised by the suite.

smells.title: README claims a LICENSE file that does not exist
smells.detail: Line 409 'MIT — see [LICENSE](LICENSE).' and line 6 badge 'MIT' link/reference a LICENSE file. `git ls-files LICENSE` returns nothing and `ls LICENSE*` => 'No such file or directory'. The repo has no LICENSE file despite asserting MIT licensing with a copyright line (411-412). This is a legal/licensing drift, not just cosmetic.

smells.title: README references a benchmarks/ directory that does not exist
smells.detail: Line 302 'Numbers will land in [`benchmarks/`](benchmarks/)' (also linked in TOC line 39 and Roadmap line 367). `ls benchmarks` => 'No such file or directory' and `git ls-files benchmarks/` is empty. The surrounding prose marks it 'in active development / coming', so this is forward-looking, but the markdown link is dead today.

smells.title: README documents .github/workflows/ci.yml but it is deleted on disk
smells.detail: README 'Project structure' line 358 lists '.github/workflows/ci.yml' and line 84 advertises 'GitHub Actions CI'. The file is git-tracked but staged-deleted: git status shows 'D .github/workflows/ci.yml' and `ls .github/workflows` => 'No such file or directory'. CI is therefore broken/removed while docs still claim it exists. Note the deletion is unstaged-vs-index inconsistent (appears as a working-tree deletion), so a commit would drop CI entirely.

smells.title: README test-count claim (104) does not match the suite (184 test functions)
smells.detail: Line 83 states '104 tests'. `grep -rcE 'def test_' tests/*.py` totals 184 test functions across 14 files (analysis 12, batch_template 9, dimensions 5, drawing 8, engineering_gear 9, engineering_keyway 5, engineering_titleblock 7, engineering_validator 9, entity_corner_ops 18, entity_creation 13, ezdxf_backend 16, layer_block 12, meta_tools 32, security 29). The number is stale by ~80 tests — the engineering/meta-tool suites were added after the README count was written.

research.topic: Recent academic work (2025-2026) on LLM/text-to-CAD, CAD program generation, LLM geometric reasoning, and parametric CAD synthesis — and how it applies to AutoCAD MCP Pro (a FastMCP server that drives AutoCAD via tool calls)
research.summary: The literature strongly validates this repo's core architectural bets and points to concrete upgrades. Three findings independently confirm the design: (1) GeoGramBench (ICLR 2026) shows frontier LLMs score <50% on translating program code into geometry at high abstraction — direct evidence that this MCP is right to forbid coordinate-guessing and force deterministic point_from_snap() (backends/base.py:535-543) and entity_select_smart() (base.py:581-591) instead of letting the LLM compute snap points from memory (CLAUDE.md Premium Rule 2). (2) The self-improving FEA-feedback agent (2026) independently arrives at this repo's exact "deterministic controller owns validation, LLM owns design" split, mirroring drawing_critique/drawing_finalize (engineering/critique.py, engineering/validator.py) being deterministic while the LLM plans; its headline result — typed closed-loop feedback beats more reasoning budget — argues for making drawing_critique a routed, iterative loop rather than a one-shot gate. (3) CADFusion (ICML 2025) shows CAD is multimodal and parametric-only supervision misses the rendering gap, which this repo only partially addresses via view_screenshot (validator step 7 emits info-only "screenshot_unavailable"). Net opportunities: add a ProCAD-style proactive clarification step in front of drawing_plan; turn the existing screenshot into an actual visual-feedback critique focus; expose a CadQuery/parametric-script tool as a higher-abstraction macro layer over the 87 low-level tools; and adopt B-Rep/primitive natural-language grounding (FutureCAD 2026) to harden entity_select_smart and the trim/fillet/chamfer corner ops (base.py:305-342) that currently require exact handle+keep-point coordinates from the LLM.

findings.title: GeoGramBench: Benchmarking the Geometric Program Reasoning in Modern LLMs
findings.takeaway: Formalizes the Program-to-Geometry task (translate drawing code into geometric reasoning). Across 17 frontier LLMs, even the best score <50% accuracy at the highest abstraction level — LLMs are fundamentally unreliable at computing geometry from procedural descriptions.
findings.relevance: Empirical justification for this MCP's no-coordinate-guessing rule. It validates routing all snap/intersection/perpendicular computation through deterministic point_from_snap() (backends/base.py:535-543) and entity_select_smart() (base.py:581-591) rather than the LLM. Argues for expanding the deterministic-tool surface (e.g. a point_intersection/point_offset helper) so the LLM never derives coordinates, and for keeping critique checks (engineering/critique.py) symbolic rather than LLM-judged.

findings.title: Self-Improving CAD Generation Agents with Finite Element Analysis as Feedback
findings.takeaway: A deterministic controller owns execution/validation/feedback-routing while the LLM owns only design decisions and code repair. Typed pass/fail reports with margins are fed back in a closed loop. One feedback round adds +13.4pp mean requirement pass; frontier models (GPT-5.5, Opus-4.7) produce ZERO first-attempt strict passes — iteration with concrete evidence beats spending more tokens on a single attempt.
findings.relevance: This is essentially this repo's architecture (deterministic drawing_critique/drawing_finalize + LLM-driven drawing_plan), independently rediscovered. Two upgrades follow: (1) make drawing_critique a routed iterative loop the agent re-enters with compact typed reports (the Issue dataclass in plan_spec.py:64-81 already carries severity/handles/detail — turn the workflow from a one-shot gate into a repair loop), and (2) the 8-step validator (engineering/validator.py) should return per-finding actionable repair hints, not just descriptions, so the LLM's next attempt is grounded.

findings.title: CADFusion: Text-to-CAD Generation Through Infusing Visual Feedback in LLMs
findings.takeaway: CAD is multimodal: a two-stage method alternates sequential learning on parametric sequences with a visual-feedback stage that rewards sequences whose RENDER looks right (rendering is many-to-one, so parametric-only supervision misses errors). Significant qualitative and quantitative gains over parametric-only baselines.
findings.relevance: This MCP renders via view_screenshot (COM Win32 capture / ezdxf matplotlib) but only uses it as an info-only validator step (engineering/validator.py:206-218 emits 'screenshot_unavailable'). Opportunity: promote rendering to a first-class visual-feedback critique focus — feed the screenshot back to the agent (or a VLM judge) to catch errors symbolic checks miss (mislabeled views, wrong proportions, crossed geometry), complementing the existing iso128/untrimmed_corner/duplicate checks in critique.py.

findings.title: Clarify Before You Draw (ProCAD): Proactive Agents for Robust Text-to-CAD Generation
findings.takeaway: A clarifying agent (finite-horizon MDP, single round, minimize interactions) audits the prompt for under-specified or conflicting dimensions BEFORE code generation, then hands a corrected spec to the coder. Drops mean Chamfer distance from 7.80 to 0.63 and invalid ratio from 4.8% to 0.9% vs a Sonnet 4.5 baseline.
findings.relevance: Maps onto the mandatory drawing_plan(intent, scale, sheet) step (CLAUDE.md Premium Rule 1, base.py:512-524). drawing_plan currently records intent passively; add a proactive validation pass that detects missing/conflicting constraints (dimensions, material, tolerance, view_count vs intent) and returns targeted clarification questions before any entity_create_* call — converting reactive mid-draw failures into one cheap up-front clarification round.

findings.title: Towards High-Fidelity CAD Generation via LLM-Driven Program Generation and Text-Based B-Rep Primitive Grounding (FutureCAD)
findings.takeaway: Lets the LLM specify geometric selections in natural language, which a BRepGround component resolves to the exact target B-Rep primitives. Specifically fixes the disconnect between parametric ops and the primitives they act on (fillets/chamfers need explicit primitive selection). SOTA via SFT+RL on a real-world CAD dataset.
findings.relevance: Directly addresses this repo's hardest LLM burden: trim/extend/fillet/chamfer (base.py:305-342) require exact cutter/boundary handles PLUS keep_x/keep_y coordinates, and entity_select_smart (base.py:581-591) uses a brittle dict predicate. A natural-language grounding layer ('fillet the top-left corner of the bracket', 'trim against the bore') resolved deterministically against the DXF/B-Rep would remove the coordinate burden GeoGramBench shows LLMs fail at, and reduce the untrimmed_corner issues critique.py:111-151 is built to catch.

findings.title: Text-to-CadQuery: A New Paradigm for CAD Generation with Scalable LLM Capabilities
findings.takeaway: Generate CadQuery (Python) code directly from text instead of low-level command sequences; the field has converged on CadQuery because Python aligns with LLMs' strong code-generation skills and gives a higher abstraction for parametric relationships and complex geometry.
findings.relevance: This MCP exposes 87 fine-grained tools; complex parts force long brittle tool-call chains. The engineering/ package (gear.py, keyway.py, titleblock.py) already proves the value of parametric Python macros over hand-drawn primitives (CLAUDE.md 'Production drawings' rule). Opportunity: add more parametric meta-tools (the gear/keyway pattern) for common features, and/or expose a sandboxed parametric-script tool so the LLM emits high-level intent that deterministic Python expands into entity_create_* calls — fewer round-trips, fewer geometry errors.

findings.title: CAD-Coder: An Open-Source Vision-Language Model for CAD Code Generation
findings.takeaway: A LLaVA-style VLM takes an image and outputs editable CadQuery code, fine-tuned on GenCAD-Code (built from Text2CAD into ~110K verified text-CadQuery-3D triplets); evaluated by compilation success rate and IoU.
findings.relevance: Suggests an image-to-drawing entry path for this MCP: a user-supplied sketch/photo of a part could seed drawing_plan and the parametric tool chain. Also models a useful eval harness — score generated drawings by automated geometric metrics (IoU/Chamfer over the rendered DXF) to regression-test the premium workflow, which the repo currently lacks.

findings.title: EvoCAD: Evolutionary CAD Code Generation with Vision Language Models
findings.takeaway: Treats CAD code generation as an evolutionary search over candidate CadQuery programs guided by VLM-evaluated renders, iterating populations toward a target shape rather than a single forward pass.
findings.relevance: An alternative to the single-pass plan->draw->critique flow: the deterministic critique/validator already provides a fitness signal. Generating a few candidate plans/geometries and selecting via critique scores (zero issues + visual fitness) could raise first-try quality for ambiguous intents, while keeping the deterministic critique as the selection oracle.

opportunities: Promote view_screenshot from an info-only validator step (engineering/validator.py:206-218) to a first-class 'visual' critique focus: render the drawing and feed it back to the agent (or a VLM judge) to catch errors symbolic checks miss — the CADFusion many-to-one rendering insight.

Add a ProCAD-style proactive clarification pass inside/in front of drawing_plan (base.py:512-524): detect under-specified or conflicting constraints (missing dims, material, tolerance, view_count vs intent) and return targeted questions BEFORE any entity_create_* call.

Turn drawing_critique into a routed iterative repair loop instead of a one-shot finalize gate: the Issue dataclass (plan_spec.py:64-81) already carries severity/handles/detail; add per-finding repair hints so each loop pass is actionable — backed by the FEA-agent result that typed closed-loop feedback beats more reasoning budget.

Expand the deterministic geometry-tool surface so the LLM never computes coordinates (GeoGramBench <50% finding): add point_intersection / point_offset / point_along helpers alongside point_from_snap (base.py:535-543), and a natural-language B-Rep/primitive grounding layer (FutureCAD) over the brittle trim/fillet/chamfer handle+keep-point API (base.py:305-342) and entity_select_smart predicate (base.py:581-591).

Add a higher-abstraction parametric layer: more engineering/ meta-tools following the gear/keyway/titleblock pattern, and/or a sandboxed parametric-script tool (CadQuery/Text-to-CadQuery style) that the LLM emits and deterministic Python expands into entity_create_* calls — fewer brittle round-trips for complex parts.

Build an automated geometric eval harness (IoU/Chamfer over rendered DXF, compilation/critique success rate) modeled on Text2CAD-Bench/CAD-Coder metrics to regression-test the premium workflow against a fixed brief set — the repo's tests/ cover units but not end-to-end drawing fidelity.

Consider candidate-generation + critique-as-fitness selection (EvoCAD pattern): generate a few plans/geometries for ambiguous intents and select the one with zero critique issues plus best visual fitness, reusing the existing deterministic critique/validator as the oracle.
research.confidence: High. WebSearch and WebFetch were both available and used; all 8 findings are real arXiv papers fetched/verified this session with titles, URLs, and dates. Codebase claims are cited to exact files/lines I read (server.py, backends/base.py, engineering/plan_spec.py, engineering/critique.py, engineering/validator.py, engineering/gear.py, CLAUDE.md). Caveat: a few arXiv IDs returned by search carry future-dated identifiers (e.g. 2602.*, 2603.*, 2605.*, 2606.*) consistent with the environment's stated date of June 2026; I fetched page content rather than relying on the ID alone, but exact quantitative figures inside abstracts were summarized by the WebFetch model and not independently re-verified line-by-line. I did not open every candidate paper from the search lists — I prioritized the ones most directly applicable to this MCP's plan-then-critique + deterministic-snap design.

research.topic: Agentic CAD loops and VLM render-critique-repair systems: techniques the AutoCAD MCP Pro project could adopt for autonomous drawing accuracy
research.summary: The project already implements the *skeleton* of an agentic drawing loop: plan-before-draw (drawing_plan → PlanSpec in engineering/plan_spec.py), deterministic snapping to avoid coordinate hallucination (point_from_snap, server.py:2384), a programmatic critique pass (engineering/critique.py: iso128/layer_color/dim_overlap/untrimmed_corner/duplicate_entities/construction_left), and an 8-step finalize validator (engineering/validator.py). The CRITICAL GAP versus the 2025 literature: the critique/validator loop is 100% symbolic/geometric — it inspects entity lists, layer tables, and endpoint coordinates. The screenshot produced at finalize (server.py:2301-2309) and probed in validator step 7 (validator.py:206-218) is written to disk and checked only for "are there bytes," but is NEVER rendered back into a VLM that compares the drawing to the stated intent (PlanSpec.intent is captured but only replayed symbolically). So the system has "draw → screenshot → check-pixels-exist" but not the "draw → render → VLM-critique → repair" closed loop that every recent paper (CADFusion, Seek-CAD, CADSmith, Physics-in-the-Loop, See-it-Say-it-Sorted, VIGA) shows is the single biggest lever on autonomous accuracy. The papers converge on a reusable recipe: (1) decompose into Planner/Coder/Executor/Validator/Refiner agents with an inner code-fix loop and an outer geometric/visual validation loop; (2) render to image, have a VLM identify 1-3 grounded discrepancies in QUALITATIVE/relative terms (not pixel coordinates), and feed them back as natural-language critique; (3) generate multiple candidate fixes and have a judge VLM pick the best vs. revert; (4) bound iterations (typically 3-10) with a sliding-window memory; (5) prefer minimal diff edits to avoid corrupting unrelated geometry; (6) where a kernel exists, drive correction from programmatic measurements rather than visual similarity (CADSmith, Physics-in-the-Loop reach 59-87% success). The project's strengths (closed critique enum, deterministic snaps, kernel-style geometric checks) align exactly with the "programmatic validator" half of these systems; what is missing is the visual/VLM half and the automated refine-and-judge orchestration. confidence high — WebSearch and WebFetch both available; arXiv IDs in the 2603/2605/2606 range are dated June 2026 (current month per env), so treat those few as very recent / possibly preprint-stage.

findings.title: CADFusion: Text-to-CAD Generation Through Infusing Visual Feedback in LLMs
findings.takeaway: Alternates a Sequential-Learning stage (train on ground-truth parametric sequences for logical coherence) with a Visual-Feedback stage that renders generated sequences and rewards those whose renders are visually preferred while penalizing bad ones — making the rendered image a first-class training/selection signal rather than an afterthought.
findings.relevance: This project renders a screenshot at finalize (server.py:2301-2309) but only checks that bytes exist (validator.py:206-218). CADFusion shows the render should drive a preference/critique signal. The repo could add a drawing_critique_visual focus that renders via the existing view_screenshot path and feeds the PNG to a VLM scored against PlanSpec.intent.

findings.title: Seek-CAD: Self-refined Generative Modeling for 3D Parametric CAD (DeepSeek-R1, training-free)
findings.takeaway: First to combine VLM visual feedback AND chain-of-thought feedback in a training-free self-refinement loop. Key idea: step-wise visual feedback (SVF) renders not just the final object but INTERMEDIATE construction states, and the VLM critiques the whole sequence so errors are caught mid-build, not only at the end.
findings.relevance: Maps directly onto this project's existing transaction/step model (transaction_begin/commit/rollback) and construction-layer scaffolding. Critique could run per-step (render after each major entity_create batch) instead of only at finalize, catching untrimmed_corner/dim_overlap before they compound. Training-free fits the MCP architecture (no model retraining needed).

findings.title: CADSmith: Multi-Agent CAD Generation with Programmatic Geometric Validation
findings.takeaway: Five-agent pipeline (Planner → Coder → Executor → Validator → Refiner) with a dual loop: inner loop fixes execution/syntax errors; outer loop measures actual geometry (edge lengths, radii, angles, face counts, hole presence, parallelism/perpendicularity/concentricity) against spec via kernel measurements and routes corrective instructions back to the Coder. Validation is programmatic, not visual.
findings.relevance: This is the closest match to the repo's current design philosophy: engineering/validator.py and engineering/critique.py ARE a programmatic Validator. What's missing is the automated Refiner agent that consumes Issue.handles/detail (plan_spec.py:64-81 already structures these) and emits targeted repair tool-calls. The repo could formalize the Validator→Refiner edge so critique issues auto-generate suggested entity_trim/entity_fillet/dimension_auto fixes.

findings.title: Physics-in-the-Loop: Hybrid Agentic Architecture for Validated CAD Engineering Design
findings.takeaway: LangGraph orchestrator with stateful cyclic nodes; three validation tiers (geometric manifold/bounds, FEA structural safety factor, VLM visual intent-match). Agents cannot override FEA results — physics is a deterministic, non-negotiable signal. Reports 81-87% FEA success, 59% hit target safety factor with FEA vs 22% without, converging in 3.3-6.0 iterations.
findings.relevance: Validates the repo's 'non-negotiable closed enum critique' philosophy (CLAUDE.md premium rules) and the 'must return [] before finalize' gate (server.py:2374). Two adoptable patterns: (1) explicit orchestrator state machine with directed cyclic edges instead of the current linear template; (2) bounded iteration budget (3-6) for the repair loop. The FEA tier is out of scope for 2D drafting, but the deterministic-signal-over-VLM-vibes principle is directly applicable to keeping geometric critique authoritative.

findings.title: See it. Say it. Sorted: Agentic System for Compositional Diagram Generation
findings.takeaway: Training-free Critic-Candidates-Judge loop for sketch→SVG. Critic VLM names 1-3 key discrepancies using QUALITATIVE spatial language ('width about half the canvas') not pixel coordinates. Multiple LLMs propose diverse candidate fixes (conservative/moderate/aggressive/alternative/focused). A Judge VLM renders all candidates and picks the best, reverting if none improve. Beat GPT-5 and Gemini-2.5-Pro on structure preservation.
findings.relevance: The qualitative-over-quantitative critique principle is a perfect complement to this project's deterministic point_from_snap (server.py:2384): use snaps for exact coordinates, use VLM only for relative/qualitative judgments it is reliable at. The Candidates+Judge pattern offers a way to make the repair step robust: instead of one blind fix, propose 3 candidate repair plans and judge the re-rendered result. Revert-on-no-improvement maps onto the existing transaction_rollback tool.

findings.title: VIGA: Vision-as-Inverse-Graphics Agent via Interleaved Multimodal Reasoning
findings.takeaway: Analysis-by-synthesis loop: generate code → render → verifier inspects from multiple viewpoints using active tools ('zoom out', 'focus on object', 'rotate'), identifies ONE different grounded issue per iteration and ties it back to code parameters; minimal line-level diff edits avoid corrupting other geometry; sliding-window memory; ~10 iterations typical, up to 100 for hard cases.
findings.relevance: Multi-view active inspection maps onto the repo's view_zoom_and_screenshot (server.py:1870) and view_zoom_window — a VLM verifier could request zoom-to-region screenshots to inspect specific Issue.handles regions rather than one global shot. The 'one grounded issue + minimal diff per iteration' discipline prevents the repair step from regressing a clean part of the drawing, complementing the duplicate_entities/untrimmed_corner detectors that already localize issues to specific handles.

findings.title: CAD-Coder: Open-Source VLM for CAD Code Generation (GenCAD-Code, 163k image-code pairs)
findings.takeaway: Fine-tuned open VLM that emits editable CadQuery from images; 100% valid-syntax rate, beats GPT-4.5 and Qwen2.5-VL-72B on 3D solid similarity; generalizes to real-world photos. Public weights/dataset at github.com/anniedoris/CAD-Coder.
findings.relevance: Demonstrates that an open, locally-runnable VLM can reliably read a rendered CAD image — meaning the visual-critique tier this project lacks does NOT require a frontier API and could run against the existing ezdxf/matplotlib render output. Could seed a 'reference image → drawing' workflow (user supplies a sketch/photo, agent reproduces it as a dimensioned DXF, then loops to verify visual match).

findings.title: Fine-Tuning Vision-Language Model for Automated Engineering Drawing Information Extraction (Florence-2 for GD&T)
findings.takeaway: Fine-tunes the open Florence-2 VLM to extract GD&T (geometric dimensioning & tolerancing) and dimension callouts directly from 2D engineering drawings — a verification primitive specialized to drafting rather than 3D solids.
findings.relevance: Directly applicable to a 2D drafting tool: a VLM that reads dimensions/GD&T off the rendered drawing enables a 'round-trip' check — extract the dimensions the VLM SEES in the render and diff them against the dimension_* entities the backend actually created. This would catch dimension_auto mis-placements (dim_overlap focus only checks proximity, not whether the value/witness lines read correctly) and fake-dimension-text (validator.py:75-100 only regex-matches text, can't confirm it visually).

opportunities: Add a visual critique focus to the closed enum (plan_spec.py CritiqueFocus + critique.py _FOCUS_DISPATCH): render via the existing view_screenshot/view_zoom_and_screenshot path, send PNG + PlanSpec.intent to a VLM, and return Issues with severity/message/handles like every other focus — closing the render→VLM→repair gap that all 7 papers identify as the top accuracy lever.

Formalize a Refiner step (CADSmith/Physics-in-the-Loop pattern): consume the structured Issue list (handles + detail already exist in plan_spec.py:64-81) and emit suggested repair tool-calls (entity_trim with keep_x/keep_y for untrimmed_corner, entity_delete for duplicates, dimension_auto for dim_overlap) instead of only reporting. Add a bounded iteration budget (3-6 per Physics-in-the-Loop's 3.3-6.0 convergence).

Move critique from finalize-only to per-step / step-wise visual feedback (Seek-CAD SVF): run lightweight critique after each major entity_create batch using the existing transaction model, so untrimmed_corner / dim_overlap are caught before they compound. Render intermediate states for the VLM, not just the final.

Adopt qualitative-over-quantitative critique split (See-it-Say-it-Sorted): keep point_from_snap (server.py:2384) authoritative for exact coordinates, restrict the VLM to relative/qualitative judgments (balance, intent-match, view completeness) it is reliable at — and keep geometric/programmatic checks as the non-overridable signal (Physics-in-the-Loop principle), matching the repo's 'non-negotiable closed enum' stance in CLAUDE.md.

Add a Candidates+Judge repair pattern: when critique fails, snapshot via transaction_begin, generate 2-3 candidate repair plans, re-render each, let a judge VLM pick the best, and transaction_rollback the losers — robustifying the repair step against single-shot regressions (the rollback infra already exists per backends/base.py _undo_stack).

Add multi-view inspection to the verifier (VIGA): drive view_zoom_window to screenshot the bounding region of specific Issue.handles so the VLM critiques localized detail instead of one global low-res shot; report 'one grounded issue + minimal diff' per iteration to avoid regressing clean geometry.

Build a dimension round-trip check (Florence-2 GD&T extraction): VLM-extract dimensions visible in the rendered drawing and diff against the actual dimension_* entities the backend created, catching mislabeled/overlapping/missing callouts that the current text-regex check (validator.py:75-100) and proximity-only dim_overlap cannot detect.

Add a reference-image-driven workflow (CAD-Coder): let a user supply a sketch/photo as the target, then run the draw→render→VLM-diff-against-target→repair loop until visual match — turning PlanSpec.intent from a text string into a visual contract; CAD-Coder shows an open local VLM suffices, so no frontier-API dependency is forced.

Use an open local VLM (CAD-Coder / Florence-2) for the visual tier so the optional visual-critique dependency mirrors the existing extras pattern in CLAUDE.md (e.g. pip install -e ".[vision]"), keeping the headless ezdxf-only install path unaffected.

research.topic: Best-in-class text-to-CAD / AI-CAD landscape vs. AutoCAD MCP Pro: UX, accuracy techniques, and feature sets to match or beat
research.summary: The leading AI-CAD products and 2025-2026 research converge on four pillars this repo only partially implements. (1) ACCURACY VIA CLOSED-LOOP VERIFICATION: the frontier is not single-pass generation+critique but iterative generate-execute-measure-refine loops. CADSmith (5-agent Planner/Coder/Executor/Validator/Refiner) reports a 38x Chamfer-distance improvement (28.37mm -> 0.74mm) and IoU 0.81 -> 0.96 by combining exact OpenCASCADE kernel measurements (volume, bbox, face/edge/vertex counts, watertightness) with an INDEPENDENT vision-language-model Judge; removing the rendered-image input alone degraded hard-case Chamfer 35x, proving numeric checks miss "false convergence." This repo's drawing_critique (engineering/critique.py) is the right instinct but is single-pass, 2D-only, and purely numeric/heuristic — no VLM judge, no measured-vs-target feedback, no refine loop. (2) PROACTIVE CLARIFICATION before drawing: ProCAD asks targeted clarifying questions when prompts are under-specified or self-conflicting, cutting invalidity 14.6% -> 0.9% and Chamfer ~80%. This repo's drawing_plan (engineering/plan_spec.py PlanSpec) commits intent but never detects ambiguity or asks. (3) PARAMETRIC, EDITABLE OUTPUT with post-hoc dimension sliders (Adam, Zoo) — users tweak dimensions without redrawing; this repo emits flat DXF entities with no parametric/constraint model. (4) DESIGN-INTENT EVALUATION beyond shape match: MUSE grades manufacturability/functionality/assemblability via rubric VLM judge; the validator.py heuristics (regex for fake dims, title-string checks) are far below this bar. The repo's genuine differentiators worth doubling down on: a real CAD backend (live AutoCAD COM + ezdxf) that most text-to-CAD tools lack, ISO-128 lineweight/layer discipline, and the snap-not-guess rule (point_from_snap) — which directly counters the #1 LLM CAD failure mode (hallucinated coordinates). Net: the repo is architecturally ahead on backends and standards but behind on the verification loop, VLM-grounded critique, clarification, and editability that define leaders.

findings.title: Zoo.dev Text-to-CAD (ML-ephant API)
findings.takeaway: Generates editable B-Rep CAD (not meshes) from text + target file format; most calls finish in 10-30s. Accuracy is highly prompt-specificity-dependent: specific dimensioned prompts ('10cm helical gear, 20 teeth') succeed; vague prompts fail. Open-source, multi-language, GPU-native geometry engine.
findings.relevance: Confirms (a) editable/parametric output is table stakes and (b) prompt specificity drives accuracy — validating this repo's drawing_plan intent-commit step, but exposing that the repo has no mechanism to PUSH BACK on vague prompts (see ProCAD).

findings.title: CADSmith: Multi-Agent CAD Generation with Programmatic Geometric Validation
findings.takeaway: 5 agents (Planner/Coder/Executor/Validator/Refiner) with dual correction loops: inner loop fixes execution errors via RAG over API docs + error patterns (3 retries); outer loop runs an INDEPENDENT Claude-Opus VLM Judge fed the prompt, code, EXACT kernel measurements (volume/bbox/face-edge-vertex counts/center-of-mass/solid validity) AND 3-view renders (5 iterations). Results: exec success 95%->100%, IoU 0.81->0.96, mean Chamfer 28.37mm->0.74mm (38x). Removing renders alone: hard-case Chamfer 1.42->49.68mm (35x worse). Refiner gets exact numeric discrepancies, never ungrounded prose.
findings.relevance: This is the single highest-leverage upgrade: turn drawing_critique into a closed refine loop and add a VLM-judge over the existing screenshot pipeline (view_screenshot already exists). The repo already produces a screenshot at finalize — feed it to a judge alongside measured-vs-PlanSpec deltas instead of discarding it. RAG-over-docs (vs fine-tune) also fits an MCP server better.

findings.title: Clarify Before You Draw (ProCAD): Proactive Agents for Robust Text-to-CAD
findings.takeaway: A clarifier agent audits the prompt for under-specification and internal conflicts, asks all targeted questions in ONE message, then emits a corrected spec for the coder. On 2,469 ambiguous prompts: invalidity 14.6%->0.9%, mean Chamfer ~80% lower. Ambiguity training data made by perturbing verified specs and keeping only perturbations that caused >=10x Chamfer degradation.
findings.relevance: drawing_plan (plan_spec.py) currently accepts whatever intent string it's given. Adding a pre-plan ambiguity/conflict check that returns clarifying questions (missing dimensions, conflicting features) before any entity_create_* would directly raise output validity — and fits the existing 'plan before draw' rule in CLAUDE.md.

findings.title: From Intent to Execution: Multimodal Chain-of-Thought RL for Precise CAD Code Generation
findings.takeaway: Combines multimodal chain-of-thought (visual+text intermediate reasoning), RL reward signals, and geometric feedback loops with self-refinement. Establishes that intermediate reasoning steps + geometric validation beat direct end-to-end translation for precision.
findings.relevance: Supports adding an explicit planning/reasoning trace and geometric reward signal. For an MCP (inference-time, not training) the actionable parallel is: structured intermediate steps + a geometric reward/score surfaced back to the model each loop, which the repo's critique could compute as a single scalar 'drawing score'.

findings.title: Adam (AdamCAD) — AI CAD Copilot, YC W25, $4.1M seed
findings.takeaway: Text-to-parametric-3D in seconds with post-generation DIMENSION SLIDERS (edit without redrawing); part-level conversational selection (click a feature, talk to it); enterprise copilot integrating Onshape/PLM/suppliers; built-in 'skills' for CAD, sourcing, and engineering documentation; applies one change across many CAD files.
findings.relevance: Two transferable UX ideas: (1) parametric editability — expose drawing parameters the user can tweak post-generation rather than flat DXF; (2) entity-level conversational editing — 'select this circle, make it the bore' maps onto the repo's handle-based model. The 'skills' framing already mirrors this repo's .claude/skills approach.

findings.title: DraftAid — 3D-to-2D production-drawing automation
findings.takeaway: Auto-converts 3D models into production-ready 2D drawings with consistent dimensions, views, and drafting standards baked in; integrates SolidWorks/Inventor; claims up to 90% drawing-time reduction. Wins the most time-intensive CAD task: fabrication drawings.
findings.relevance: Directly adjacent to this repo's 2D engineering output. The standards-enforcement + auto-dimensioning + multi-view layout is exactly what dimension_auto and titleblock tools aim at — but DraftAid's auto-view-generation and full standards pass set the bar the repo's validator/critique should aspire to.

findings.title: CADGPT — in-tool AutoCAD copilot
findings.takeaway: Context-aware chat embedded in the CAD UI that writes AutoLISP/Python automation and understands CAD-specific commands/terminology; augments rather than replaces the drafter.
findings.relevance: Validates this repo's COM-only system_run_lisp / system_run_command surface as a real product category. A safe, sandboxed LISP/script-generation path (the repo already has security.py) is a competitive feature, not just plumbing.

findings.title: Autodesk Project Bernini + 'neural CAD' + Autodesk Assistant
findings.takeaway: Bernini generates FUNCTIONAL 3D geometry (shape and texture decoupled) from text/image/voxel/point-cloud, trained on 10M shapes; 'neural CAD' foundation models aim to automate 80-90% of routine design; Autodesk Assistant (coming to AutoCAD/Revit/Inventor) demoed AUTOMATED COMPLIANCE/STANDARDS CHECKING in AutoCAD.
findings.relevance: The incumbent is shipping an in-AutoCAD AI assistant whose flagship demo is automated compliance checking — exactly the niche of this repo's drawing_critique/validator. To 'beat' it the repo should make standards compliance (ISO-128, layer/lineweight, GD&T) its sharpest, most-cited capability.

findings.title: MUSE: Benchmarking Manufacturable, Functional, and Assemblable Text-to-CAD
findings.takeaway: Argues geometric-similarity metrics miss real design quality; introduces a 3-stage protocol (code check -> geometric check -> design-intent alignment) where the final stage uses design-specific rubrics + a rubric-based VLM judge (human-validated) to score functionality, manufacturability, assemblability.
findings.relevance: A blueprint for upgrading validator.py: replace ad-hoc regex/title heuristics with a staged protocol whose final stage is a rubric VLM judge over the rendered drawing. Also defines what 'good' means beyond 'it drew lines.'

findings.title: Text-to-CAD evaluation metric suite (CadBench/BenchCAD/surveys)
findings.takeaway: Standard metric suite: volumetric IoU, surface IoU, Chamfer distance, valid-shape rate (Invalidity Ratio = % that can't execute/render), plus token/operation counts; surface-IoU match threshold ~1% of bbox diagonal. Validity and design-intent alignment are emphasized over pure shape resemblance.
findings.relevance: The repo has no quantitative quality metric — drawing_critique returns a list of issues but no score. Adopting an Invalidity-Ratio-style pass/fail plus a 2D analog of IoU/Chamfer (against the PlanSpec or a reference DXF) gives a regression-testable accuracy number for the smoke suite (scripts/smoke_premium.py).

opportunities: Close the loop: convert drawing_critique (engineering/critique.py) from single-pass into an iterative generate->measure->refine cycle, returning measured-vs-PlanSpec numeric deltas (not just issue strings) so the model can self-correct — mirrors CADSmith's outer loop that drove Chamfer 28.37->0.74mm.

Add a VLM-judge focus to drawing_critique that consumes the already-produced view_screenshot bytes + the PlanSpec + measured geometry and judges intent-match. CADSmith showed dropping the visual channel made hard cases 35x worse; the repo already renders a screenshot at finalize but throws away its judgment value.

Add a pre-plan clarification step to drawing_plan (plan_spec.py): detect under-specified/conflicting intent and return targeted clarifying questions in one batch before any entity_create_* runs (ProCAD: invalidity 14.6%->0.9%).

Introduce a single scalar 'drawing score' / Invalidity-Ratio pass-fail from critique+validator so quality is regression-testable in scripts/smoke_premium.py — today there is no quantitative accuracy metric anywhere in the repo.

Make standards-compliance the flagship capability (Autodesk's own AutoCAD-AI demo is compliance checking): broaden critique beyond the 6 closed focuses to GD&T placement, datum/feature-control-frame validity, drawing-frame/scale consistency, and view-projection checks — areas no surveyed text-to-CAD product covers for 2D.

Add parametric editability: capture drawing parameters (driving dimensions) in the PlanSpec and expose a tool to re-drive them so users tweak a value and regenerate, matching Adam/Zoo's editable-output expectation instead of emitting flat one-shot DXF.

Add entity-level conversational editing leveraging the existing handle model ('select this circle -> make it the 20mm bore'), the UX paradigm Adam highlights, using entity_select_smart + point_from_snap which already exist.

Adopt RAG-over-docs for error recovery rather than relying on model memory: index ezdxf/AutoCAD-COM error patterns and tool-usage docs so failed tool calls get retried with grounded fixes (CADSmith inner loop, 3 retries) — fits an MCP server better than fine-tuning.

Generalize the 8-step validator.py beyond gear/keyway-specific regex/title heuristics into a MUSE-style staged protocol (code/exec check -> geometric check -> rubric VLM intent check) so it scales to arbitrary drawings, not just the engineering primitives currently hardcoded.

Double down on the snap-not-guess rule (point_from_snap) and surface it as a marketed accuracy feature: hallucinated coordinates are the dominant LLM-CAD failure mode the literature works hard to fix, and the repo already structurally prevents it — make critique flag any geometry whose endpoints were not snap-derived.
research.confidence: High. WebSearch and WebFetch were both available and used across 7 searches + 4 fetches. All product/research claims are cited to primary sources (arXiv papers, vendor pages, TechCrunch, AEC Magazine). Repo gap claims are grounded in files I read directly: engineering/critique.py (single-pass, 2D-only, numeric/heuristic, 6 closed focuses), engineering/plan_spec.py (PlanSpec commits intent, no ambiguity detection), engineering/validator.py (regex/title-string heuristics, gear-specific), and server.py tool registrations (point_from_snap, dimension_auto, entity_select_smart, view_screenshot exist).
